 {"id":"franken_networkx-5xp","title":"Predicates: find_minimal_d_separator, is_valid_directed_joint_degree","description":"## Scope
Implement 2 predicate/separator functions:
- find_minimal_d_separator(G, u, v) — find a minimal d-separating set between node sets u and v in a DAG (Bayesian network criterion). Algorithm: (1) compute ancestral subgraph of u ∪ v ∪ ancestors, (2) moralize (connect co-parents), (3) find minimum vertex cut in moralized graph, (4) iteratively remove nodes from cut while maintaining separation.
- is_valid_directed_joint_degree(joint_degrees) — validate a directed joint degree dictionary: check that sum of (d_in * count) = sum of (d_out * count) for all entries, and that the degree constraints are mutually consistent. This determines if a directed graph with the given joint degree distribution can exist.

## Implementation Strategy
- find_minimal_d_separator: compose over existing ancestors(), is_d_separator(), and graph operations
- is_valid_directed_joint_degree: pure arithmetic validation on the degree dictionary

## Test Requirements
- find_minimal_d_separator: result is a d-separator AND minimal (no proper subset is a separator)
- is_valid_directed_joint_degree: returns True for known-valid sequences, False for invalid
- NX cross-validation on classic Bayesian network structures (chains, forks, colliders)","status":"closed","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:34:38.965937921Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T22:43:05.528211525Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T22:43:05.527556025Z","close_reason":"Implemented 2 predicates: find_minimal_d_separator, is_valid_directed_joint_degree.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms","predicates"]}

On a simple DiGraph, NetworkX returns the expected reversed subgraph edges and copy() materializes a DiGraph. Match the upstream subgraph/copy behavior.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T07:08:33.331738246Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T07:08:33.331738246Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"franken_networkx-64l","title":"Graph Utilities: Voronoi cells, Stoer-Wagner, dedensify, quotient graph","description":"## Scope
Implement ~10 graph utility functions:
- voronoi_cells(G, center_nodes, weight=None): partition graph nodes into Voronoi cells based on shortest path distances to center nodes. Returns dict mapping center -> set of closest nodes. O(V * Dijkstra).
- stoer_wagner(G, weight=None, heap=None): find global minimum cut using Stoer-Wagner algorithm. Returns (cut_value, partition). O(V * E + V^2 * log(V)) with Fibonacci heap, O(V^3) with binary heap. Much faster than max-flow for undirected graphs.
- dedensify(G, threshold, prefix=None, copy=True): reduce dense bipartite substructures by introducing auxiliary nodes. Reduces edge count while preserving reachability.
- quotient_graph(G, partition, edge_relation=None, node_data=None, edge_data=None, relabel=True, create_using=None): contract partition blocks into single nodes. Edge between blocks if any cross-block edge exists.
- snap_aggregation(G, node_attributes, edge_attributes=None): SNAP-based graph summarization — group nodes by attribute value, aggregate edges.
- full_join(G, H, rename=(None,None)): complete bipartite join of G and H (all edges between G and H, plus internal edges preserved).
- identified_nodes(G, u, v, self_loops=True): alias for contracted_nodes.
- inverse_line_graph(G): reconstruct the original graph from its line graph. NP-hard in general but polynomial for specific cases (Whitney's theorem).

## Test Requirements
- voronoi_cells: verify every node assigned to nearest center, verify complete partition
- stoer_wagner: verify cut value matches NX minimum_cut_value, verify partition is valid
- dedensify: verify reachability preserved
- quotient_graph: verify contracted structure matches partition
- NX cross-validation on all functions","status":"closed","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T02:19:47.823910183Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T07:29:28.208587645Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T07:29:28.208044537Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms","tier-4"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-deh","title":"Drawing & Visualization: draw_networkx variants, LaTeX export, network_text","description":"## Scope
Implement ~15 drawing/visualization functions:
- `draw_networkx` — main drawing function (matplotlib)
- `draw_networkx_nodes` — draw nodes only
- `draw_networkx_edges` — draw edges only
- `draw_networkx_labels` — draw node labels
- `draw_networkx_edge_labels` — draw edge labels
- `draw_circular` — draw with circular layout
- `draw_kamada_kawai` — draw with Kamada-Kawai layout
- `draw_planar` — draw with planar layout
- `draw_random` — draw with random positions
- `draw_shell` — draw with shell layout
- `draw_spectral` — draw with spectral layout
- `draw_spring` — draw with spring layout
- `draw_forceatlas2` — draw with ForceAtlas2 layout
- `write_latex` / `to_latex` / `to_latex_raw` — export graph to LaTeX/TikZ
- `network_text` — ASCII art representation of graph

## Implementation Approach
- Most draw_* functions delegate to matplotlib with layout computation
- Can wrap NetworkX drawing directly for many of these
- network_text: ASCII tree/graph rendering, pure Python
- LaTeX export: generate TikZ code, pure Python string generation

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/drawing/`

## Test Requirements
- Verify draw functions don't error on valid graphs
- Verify LaTeX output is valid TikZ
- Verify network_text produces readable output
- Visual regression tests (optional)","status":"closed","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:14:33.237335091Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T03:36:38.812894650Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T03:36:38.812214806Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["drawing","tier-4","visualization"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_networkx-deh","depends_on_id":"franken_networkx-l85","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:16:02.736514279Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-m1r","title":"I/O Format Expansion: GEXF, Pajek, LEDA, sparse6/graph6, adjlist variants","description":"## Scope
Implement ~26 I/O functions across multiple graph file formats:

### GEXF Format
- `read_gexf` / `write_gexf` — Graph Exchange XML Format (used by Gephi)
- `generate_gexf` — generate GEXF lines
- `parse_gexf` — parse GEXF string

### Pajek Format
- `read_pajek` / `write_pajek` — Pajek .net format
- `parse_pajek` — parse Pajek string
- `generate_pajek` — generate Pajek lines

### LEDA Format
- `read_leda` / `write_leda` — LEDA native format
- `parse_leda` — parse LEDA string

### sparse6 / graph6
- `from_sparse6_bytes` / `to_sparse6_bytes`
- `from_graph6_bytes` / `to_graph6_bytes`
- `read_sparse6` / `write_sparse6`
- `read_graph6` / `write_graph6`

### Adjlist / Edgelist Variants
- `read_multiline_adjlist` / `write_multiline_adjlist`
- `read_weighted_edgelist` / `write_weighted_edgelist`
- `parse_adjlist` / `generate_adjlist`
- `parse_edgelist` / `generate_edgelist`

## Implementation Location
- Rust: `crates/fnx-readwrite/src/` — new format modules for performance
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/readwrite/` — bindings and format-specific wrappers

## Approach
- Can be implemented incrementally by format family
- sparse6/graph6 are compact binary encodings — Rust implementation recommended
- GEXF is XML-based — use quick-xml crate in Rust
- Pajek/LEDA are line-based text formats — straightforward parsing

## Edge Cases
- Unicode node labels
- Attribute preservation (GEXF has rich attribute support)
- Multigraph encoding/decoding
- Large file streaming (don't load entire file into memory)
- Malformed input handling with clear error messages

## Test Requirements
- Round-trip tests: write then read should produce identical graph
- Cross-validate against NetworkX for each format
- Test with real-world graph files from SNAP/KONECT datasets
- Malformed input rejection tests","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:12:15.592143950Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:53.678999365Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:53.678553020Z","close_reason":"All sub-beads closed: GEXF (omw), Pajek/LEDA (l34), sparse6/graph6 (guk), parse/generate (fh8). I/O format expansion complete.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["io","readwrite","tier-2"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-tnl","title":"Utility Extras A: link prediction, attribute correlation, walk counting","description":"Implement ~10 utility functions: cn_soundarajan_hopcroft(G, ebunch, community) — Common Neighbor link prediction with community info; ra_index_soundarajan_hopcroft(G, ebunch, community) — Resource Allocation with community; cd_index(G, node, timestep) — consolidation-diffusion index; communicability_exp(G) — communicability via scipy.linalg.expm (more accurate than eigendecomposition); node_attribute_xy(G, attribute) — yield (x,y) pairs of attribute values across edges; node_degree_xy(G, x, y, weight, nodes) — yield (degree_x, degree_y) pairs; number_of_walks(G, walk_length) — count walks of given length via matrix power; mixing_dict(G, mapping, normalized) — generic mixing dictionary; local_constraint(G, u, v) — Burt's local constraint for a specific edge; recursive_simple_cycles(G) — find all simple cycles (recursive variant).
